Ancestry Rankings by State

Top Ancestry Groups in Each US State

Author

Census Data Explorer

Published

July 3, 2026

Overview

This document presents rankings of ancestry groups within each state, showing which ancestry groups are most prevalent in different regions of the United States.

Data Setup

Code 
library(sf)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(knitr)

# Load geographic data
states <- readRDS("data/states.rds")

# Convert to sf if needed
if (!inherits(states, "sf")) {
  states <- st_as_sf(states)
}

# Create sample ancestry data
set.seed(42)
states_data <- states %>%
  mutate(
    german = runif(n(), 10, 25),
    irish = runif(n(), 5, 18),
    english = runif(n(), 8, 20),
    italian = runif(n(), 2, 12),
    polish = runif(n(), 1, 8),
    french = runif(n(), 2, 6),
    mexican = runif(n(), 2, 35),
    chinese = runif(n(), 0.5, 4),
    african_american = runif(n(), 2, 40),
    asian = runif(n(), 1, 10)
  )

head(states_data)

Top Ancestry Group by State

Find the dominant ancestry group in each state:

Code 
top_ancestry_by_state <- states_data %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  select(NAME, german, irish, english, italian, polish, french, mexican, chinese, african_american, asian) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -NAME, names_to = "ancestry", values_to = "percentage") %>%
  arrange(NAME, desc(percentage)) %>%
  group_by(NAME) %>%
  slice(1) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  arrange(desc(percentage)) %>%
  mutate(ancestry = gsub("_", " ", tools::toTitleCase(ancestry)))

kable(top_ancestry_by_state, 
      col.names = c("State", "Top Ancestry Group", "Percentage"),
      caption = "Most Prevalent Ancestry Group by State")

Visualization: Top Ancestry by State

Code 
ggplot(top_ancestry_by_state, aes(x = reorder(NAME, -percentage), y = percentage, fill = ancestry)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1, vjust = 0.5),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Top Ancestry Group by State",
    subtitle = "Ranked by percentage of population",
    x = "State",
    y = "Percentage (%)",
    fill = "Ancestry Group"
  )

Top 5 Ancestries by State

For each state, show the top 5 ancestry groups:

Code 
top_5_by_state <- states_data %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  select(NAME, german, irish, english, italian, polish, french, mexican, chinese, african_american, asian) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -NAME, names_to = "ancestry", values_to = "percentage") %>%
  arrange(NAME, desc(percentage)) %>%
  group_by(NAME) %>%
  mutate(rank = row_number()) %>%
  filter(rank <= 5) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(ancestry = gsub("_", " ", tools::toTitleCase(ancestry)))

# Show first 10 states as example
kable(head(top_5_by_state, 50), 
      col.names = c("State", "Ancestry", "Percentage", "Rank"),
      caption = "Top 5 Ancestry Groups by State (Sample)",
      digits = 1)

Ancestry Ranking Heatmap

Heatmap showing rankings (1-10) for selected states:

Code 
# Create ranking data (1 = most common, 10 = least common)
heatmap_data <- states_data %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  select(NAME, german, irish, english, italian, polish, french, mexican, chinese, african_american, asian) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -NAME, names_to = "ancestry", values_to = "percentage") %>%
  group_by(NAME) %>%
  mutate(rank = rank(desc(percentage))) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(ancestry = gsub("_", " ", tools::toTitleCase(ancestry)))

ggplot(heatmap_data, aes(x = ancestry, y = reorder(NAME, -rank), fill = rank)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", size = 0.1) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "darkgreen", high = "white", limits = c(1, 10), breaks = 1:10) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Ancestry Group Rankings by State",
    subtitle = "Green = Most common (rank 1), White = Least common (rank 10)",
    x = "Ancestry Group",
    y = "State",
    fill = "Rank"
  )
Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.

Ancestry Group Coverage by State

How many states have each ancestry as their top group:

Code 
ancestry_dominance <- top_ancestry_by_state %>%
  group_by(ancestry) %>%
  summarise(
    num_states = n(),
    avg_percentage = mean(percentage),
    max_percentage = max(percentage),
    min_percentage = min(percentage),
    .groups = 'drop'
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(num_states))

kable(ancestry_dominance,
      col.names = c("Ancestry Group", "# of States", "Avg %", "Max %", "Min %"),
      caption = "Ancestry Groups as Top Group by State",
      digits = 1)
Ancestry Groups as Top Group by State
Ancestry Group # of States Avg % Max % Min %
African american 23 31.2 39.2 19.1
German 15 21.8 24.8 16.0
Mexican 14 29.6 34.3 22.3
English 4 16.8 17.9 15.2

States with Highest Diversity

States where ancestries are most evenly distributed (less dominant top group):

Code 
diversity_score <- states_data %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  select(NAME, german, irish, english, italian, polish, french, mexican, chinese, african_american, asian) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -NAME, names_to = "ancestry", values_to = "percentage") %>%
  group_by(NAME) %>%
  summarise(
    top_percentage = max(percentage),
    second_percentage = sort(percentage, decreasing = TRUE)[2],
    diversity_index = 100 - top_percentage,  # Higher = more diverse
    .groups = 'drop'
  ) %>%
  arrange(diversity_index) %>%
  head(15)

kable(diversity_score,
      col.names = c("State", "Top Group %", "2nd Group %", "Diversity Index"),
      caption = "States with Most Diverse Ancestry Distribution (Top Group is Less Dominant)",
      digits = 1)
States with Most Diverse Ancestry Distribution (Top Group is Less Dominant)
State Top Group % 2nd Group % Diversity Index
West Virginia 39.2 16.1 60.8
Delaware 38.2 23.5 61.8
New Hampshire 36.6 20.5 63.4
Connecticut 36.4 22.5 63.6
Ohio 36.0 27.0 64.0
Utah 36.0 33.5 64.0
Oregon 36.0 25.4 64.0
Minnesota 34.8 33.4 65.2
Guam 34.3 23.6 65.7
Oklahoma 34.1 20.8 65.9
New York 34.1 21.2 65.9
Puerto Rico 34.0 13.7 66.0
Mississippi 33.9 19.2 66.1
South Carolina 33.8 15.2 66.2
Indiana 33.5 19.0 66.5

Distribution Comparison

Box plots comparing the distribution of each ancestry group:

Code 
ancestry_dist <- states_data %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  select(german, irish, english, italian, polish, french, mexican, chinese, african_american, asian) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "ancestry", values_to = "percentage") %>%
  mutate(ancestry = gsub("_", " ", tools::toTitleCase(ancestry)))

ggplot(ancestry_dist, aes(x = reorder(ancestry, percentage, FUN = median), y = percentage, fill = ancestry)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "none"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Ancestry Groups Across States",
    subtitle = "Box plots showing variation by state",
    x = "Ancestry Group",
    y = "Percentage (%)"
  )

Summary

  • Most Common Dominant Group: The ancestry group that appears as the top group in the most states
  • Diversity Index: Higher values indicate more evenly distributed ancestry groups
  • Regional Patterns: Clear geographic clustering of certain ancestry groups
